headline: Intensive outpatient program — medical necessity (UHC)
denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

We received your provider's request on 08/22/2026 for Intensive outpatient program (IOP) for depression related to Major depressive disorder, recurrent, moderate (F33.1). After review, we cannot approve this request.

Our review applied UnitedHealthcare Medical Management Guidelines and Optum clinical criteria. The documentation submitted does not demonstrate that the service is medically necessary for this member at this time. A lower level of care is appropriate for Major depressive disorder, recurrent, moderate (F33.1); Intensive outpatient program (IOP) for depression exceeds what is required. Outpatient management has not been exhausted per clinical guidelines.

Specifically, the information provided does not satisfy the utilization-management requirements associated with level of care too high. The clinical notes reviewed did not include sufficient detail to confirm that plan criteria were met. The requesting provider may submit additional records, but the current file does not support approval.

If your provider wishes to discuss this determination, they may contact our physician review line. Any discussion must occur within the timeframe described in your plan materials.

You have the right to appeal this determination. To file a standard appeal, send a written request and supporting medical records within 180 days of the date of this letter. Include your member identification number and the service reference shown on your explanation of benefits.

Sincerely,

UnitedHealthcare Utilization Management

This determination was made by Dr. J. Smith, Medical Director.
clinical_context: The 19-year-old member has Major depressive disorder, recurrent, moderate (F33.1). She has been treated under commercial coverage with Intensive outpatient program (IOP) for depression recommended as the next appropriate step. PHQ-9 scores 19 and 21 in Q4 2025 and Q1 2026; failed sertraline 200 mg ×14 weeks and bupropion XL 300 mg ×12 weeks. This directly contradicts the insurer's position that level of care too high bars coverage: the chart documents dates, doses, and objective findings that were submitted with the original request but are not reflected in the denial letter's rationale. Treating clinicians note ongoing functional impairment and avoidable harm if her care is delayed further.
